text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic splenectomy, beginning with the dissection of ligaments surrounding a large recurrent cyst in the spleen. The peritoneal attachments are freed, and the cyst is visibly emptied during the procedure.
This cyst was recurrent, though it was aspirated five times ultrasonographically. It filled up every time and gave a lot of discomfort to the patient.

The surgeon uses an ERBI bipolar radio frequency guided instrument to dissect and cut the splenic artery, freeing the spleen from its attachments to the tail of the pancreas. The splenic artery is visible, standing separately as it is dissected. A monopolar hook is then used to dissect the diaphragmatic attachments of the spleen, completing its liberation.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic splenectomy, dissecting the remaining splenic attachments from the diaphragm. The spleen and a perisplenic giant cyst are removed through a small incision. The pulsating splenic artery is visible during the procedure.
